name: blinker

metadata.name: blinker
metadata.summary: Fast, simple object-to-object and broadcast signaling

documentation:
# Blinker

Blinker provides a fast dispatching system that allows any number of
interested parties to subscribe to events, or "signals".


## Pallets Community Ecosystem

> [!IMPORTANT]\
> This project is part of the Pallets Community Ecosystem. Pallets is the open
> source organization that maintains Flask; Pallets-Eco enables community
> maintenance of related projects. If you are interested in helping maintain
> this project, please reach out on [the Pallets Discord server][discord].
>
> [discord]: https://discord.gg/pallets


## Example

Signal receivers can subscribe to specific senders or receive signals
sent by any sender.

```pycon
>>> from blinker import signal
>>> started = signal('round-started')
>>> def each(round):
...     print(f"Round {round}")
...
>>> started.connect(each)

>>> def round_two(round):
...     print("This is round two.")
...
>>> started.connect(round_two, sender=2)

>>> for round in range(1, 4):
...     started.send(round)
...
Round 1!
Round 2!
This is round two.
Round 3!
```

functions.name: blinker._utilities.make_id
documentation:
Get a stable identifier for a receiver or sender, to be used as a dict
key or in a set.

classes.name: blinker._utilities.Symbol
documentation:
A constant symbol, nicer than `object()`. Repeated calls return the
same instance.

```pycon
>>> Symbol('foo') is Symbol('foo')
True
>>> Symbol('foo')
foo
```

variables.name: blinker.base.ANY
documentation: Symbol for "any sender".

variables.name: blinker.base.default_namespace
documentation:
A default {class}`Namespace` for creating named signals. {func}`signal`
creates a {class}`NamedSignal` in this namespace.

variables.name: blinker.base.signal
documentation:
Return a {class}`NamedSignal` in {data}`default_namespace` with the given
`name`, creating it if required. Repeated calls with the same name return the
same signal.

classes.name: blinker.base.Signal

methods.name: blinker.base.Signal.receiver_connected
documentation:
Emitted at the end of each {meth}`connect` call.

The signal sender is the signal instance, and the {meth}`connect`
arguments are passed through: `receiver`, `sender`, and `weak`.

:::{versionadded} 1.2
:::

methods.name: blinker.base.Signal.receiver_disconnected
documentation:
Emitted at the end of each {meth}`disconnect` call.

The sender is the signal instance, and the {meth}`disconnect` arguments
are passed through: `receiver` and `sender`.

This signal is emitted **only** when {meth}`disconnect` is called
explicitly. This signal cannot be emitted by an automatic disconnect
when a weakly referenced receiver or sender goes out of scope, as the
instance is no longer be available to be used as the sender for this
signal.

An alternative approach is available by subscribing to
{attr}`receiver_connected` and setting up a custom weakref cleanup
callback on weak receivers and senders.

:::{versionadded} 1.2
:::

methods.name: blinker.base.Signal.connect
documentation:
Connect `receiver` to be called when the signal is sent by
`sender`.

```
param receiver

The callable to call when  is called with
the given sender, passing sender as a positional argument
along with any extra keyword arguments.

param sender

Any object or . receiver will only be
called when  is called with this sender. If ANY, the
receiver will be called for any sender. A receiver may be connected
to multiple senders by calling  multiple times.

param weak

Track the receiver with a . The receiver will
be automatically disconnected when it is garbage collected. When
connecting a receiver defined within a function, set to False,
otherwise it will be disconnected when the function scope ends.
```

methods.name: blinker.base.Signal.connect_via
documentation:
Connect the decorated function to be called when the signal is sent
by `sender`.

The decorated function will be called when {meth}`send` is called with
the given `sender`, passing `sender` as a positional argument along
with any extra keyword arguments.

```
param sender

Any object or . receiver will only be
called when  is called with this sender. If ANY, the
receiver will be called for any sender. A receiver may be connected
to multiple senders by calling  multiple times.

param weak

Track the receiver with a . The receiver will
be automatically disconnected when it is garbage collected. When
connecting a receiver defined within a function, set to False,
otherwise it will be disconnected when the function scope ends.=
```

:::{versionadded} 1.1
:::

methods.name: blinker.base.Signal.connected_to
documentation:
A context manager that temporarily connects `receiver` to the
signal while a `with` block executes. When the block exits, the
receiver is disconnected. Useful for tests.

```
param receiver

The callable to call when  is called with
the given sender, passing sender as a positional argument
along with any extra keyword arguments.

param sender

Any object or . receiver will only be
called when  is called with this sender. If ANY, the
receiver will be called for any sender.
```

:::{versionadded} 1.1
:::

methods.name: blinker.base.Signal.muted
documentation:
A context manager that temporarily disables the signal. No receivers
will be called if the signal is sent, until the `with` block exits.
Useful for tests.

methods.name: blinker.base.Signal.send

type.name:
c.Callable[
        [c.Callable[..., c.Coroutine[t.Any, t.Any, t.Any]]], c.Callable[..., t.Any]
    ]
    | None

documentation:
Call all receivers that are connected to the given `sender`
or {data}`ANY`. Each receiver is called with `sender` as a positional
argument along with any extra keyword arguments. Return a list of
`(receiver, return value)` tuples.

The order receivers are called is undefined, but can be influenced by
setting {attr}`set_class`.

If a receiver raises an exception, that exception will propagate up.
This makes debugging straightforward, with an assumption that correctly
implemented receivers will not raise.

```
param sender

Call receivers connected to this sender, in addition to
those connected to .

param _async_wrapper

Will be called on any receivers that are async
coroutines to turn them into sync callables. For example, could run
the receiver with an event loop.

param kwargs

Extra keyword arguments to pass to each receiver.
```

:::{versionchanged} 1.7 Added the ``_async_wrapper`` argument.
:::

methods.name: blinker.base.Signal.send_async

type.name:
c.Callable[
        [c.Callable[..., t.Any]], c.Callable[..., c.Coroutine[t.Any, t.Any, t.Any]]
    ]
    | None

documentation:
Await all receivers that are connected to the given `sender`
or {data}`ANY`. Each receiver is called with `sender` as a positional
argument along with any extra keyword arguments. Return a list of
`(receiver, return value)` tuples.

The order receivers are called is undefined, but can be influenced by
setting {attr}`set_class`.

If a receiver raises an exception, that exception will propagate up.
This makes debugging straightforward, with an assumption that correctly
implemented receivers will not raise.

```
param sender

Call receivers connected to this sender, in addition to
those connected to .

param _sync_wrapper

Will be called on any receivers that are sync
callables to turn them into async coroutines. For example,
could call the receiver in a thread.

param kwargs

Extra keyword arguments to pass to each receiver.
```

:::{versionadded} 1.7
:::

methods.name: blinker.base.Signal.has_receivers_for
documentation:
Check if there is at least one receiver that will be called with the
given `sender`. A receiver connected to {data}`ANY` will always be
called, regardless of sender. Does not check if weakly referenced
receivers are still live. See {meth}`receivers_for` for a stronger
search.

```
param sender

Check for receivers connected to this sender, in addition
to those connected to .
```

methods.name: blinker.base.Signal.receivers_for
documentation:
Yield each receiver to be called for `sender`, in addition to those
to be called for {data}`ANY`. Weakly referenced receivers that are not
live will be disconnected and skipped.

```
param sender

Yield receivers connected to this sender, in addition
to those connected to .
```

methods.name: blinker.base.Signal.disconnect
documentation:
Disconnect `receiver` from being called when the signal is sent by
`sender`.

```
param receiver

A connected receiver callable.

param sender

Disconnect from only this sender. By default, disconnect
from all senders.
```

methods.name: blinker.base.Signal._make_cleanup_receiver
documentation:
Create a callback function to disconnect a weakly referenced
receiver when it is garbage collected.

methods.name: blinker.base.Signal._make_cleanup_sender
documentation:
Create a callback function to disconnect all receivers for a weakly
referenced sender when it is garbage collected.

methods.name: blinker.base.Signal._cleanup_bookkeeping
documentation:
Prune unused sender/receiver bookkeeping. Not threadsafe.

Connecting & disconnecting leaves behind a small amount of bookkeeping
data. Typical workloads using Blinker, for example in most web apps,
Flask, CLI scripts, etc., are not adversely affected by this
bookkeeping.

With a long-running process performing dynamic signal routing with high
volume, e.g. connecting to function closures, senders are all unique
object instances. Doing all of this over and over may cause memory usage
to grow due to extraneous bookkeeping. (An empty `set` for each stale
sender/receiver pair.)

This method will prune that bookkeeping away, with the caveat that such
pruning is not threadsafe. The risk is that cleanup of a fully
disconnected receiver/sender pair occurs while another thread is
connecting that same pair. If you are in the highly dynamic, unique
receiver/sender situation that has lead you to this method, that failure
mode is perhaps not a big deal for you.

methods.name: blinker.base.Signal._clear_state
documentation: Disconnect all receivers and senders. Useful for tests.

class_variables.name: blinker.base.Signal.ANY
documentation: An alias for the {data}`~blinker.ANY` sender symbol.

class_variables.name: blinker.base.Signal.set_class
documentation:
The set class to use for tracking connected receivers and senders.
Python's `set` is unordered. If receivers must be dispatched in the order
they were connected, an ordered set implementation can be used.

:::{versionadded} 1.7
:::

A notification emitter.

```
param doc

The docstring for the signal.
```

classes.name: blinker.base.NamedSignal
documentation:
A named generic notification emitter. The name is not used by the signal
itself, but matches the key in the {class}`Namespace` that it belongs to.

```
param name

The name of the signal within the namespace.

param doc

The docstring for the signal.
```

classes.name: blinker.base.Namespace

methods.name: blinker.base.Namespace.signal
documentation:
Return the {class}`NamedSignal` for the given `name`, creating it
if required. Repeated calls with the same name return the same signal.

```
param name

The name of the signal.

param doc

The docstring of the signal.
```
A dict mapping names to signals.
